title: 6 The OAS Fraud Analyst Role
body_text:
6	The OAS Fraud Analyst Role
6.1 	Overview of the Fraud Analyst Role
The OAS Fraud Analyst role allows authorized users to manage their own
Interac
e-Transfer
Â®
fraud incidents. The role has similar searching and viewing capabilities as the OAS CSR role, but with additional functionality useful in investigating and actioning, where applicable, fraud-suspicious transactions.
Use of the OAS Fraud Analyst role is available upon request. However, you must provide Central 1 with a signed OAS Terms of Use agreement that governs your use of the role.
Once an institution is approved for the OAS Fraud Analyst role, Central 1 upgrades OAS CSR users to the OAS Fraud Analyst role upon request. These users must be set up as OAS CSRs before they can be promoted to the OAS Fraud Analyst role.
Note:	Financial institutions interested in implementing the OAS Fraud Analyst role must email Central 1âs FAS team and request to be approved for this role. FAS coordinates with them for the execution and return of a Terms of Use agreement. Requests for promoting individual OAS CSR Users to the role of OAS Fraud Analyst must be sent to the FAS team after the institution is approved to use this role. See Section 1.2, Contacts, Websites, and Other Resources, for FAS contact information.
6.2 	Viewing Fraud-suspicious Transactions
Your financial institution receives notifications about
Interac
e-Transfer transactions that
Interac
Â®
considers fraud-suspicious regardless of whether you are the sending or the receiving financial institution.
In the case of outgoing transfers (or transfer requests), notifications are sent as alerts sent through the Central 1 fraud management system (FAS or EFM) you use.
In the case of incoming transfers, Central 1 forwards to you any email notifications we receive from
Interac,
after
Interac
determines who the receiving financial institution is (i.e., when a recipient accepts a transfer or attempts to accept a transfer, or when a transfer settles automatically, such as those settled through Autodeposit or Account Number Routing).
Using Basic Searches
Email notifications, and Central 1 alerts, about suspicious transactions contain
Interac
reference numbers (an
Interac
Payment Reference or an
Interac
Request Reference). You can use these reference numbers to search and view the transactionâs details using the basic search functionality in OAS. See Section 4.2, Basic Searches in OAS.
Using Reported Transfers
Outgoing transactions reported by
Interac
as fraud-suspicious can also be found on the Report Transfers section of the Case Management page (available to OAS Fraud Analysts).
An OAS Fraud Analyst can view reported transactions in the Reported Transfers section as follows:
Log in to OAS as described in Section 3.5, Logging in to OAS.
Expand the
Reported Transfers
section on the Case Management page. See Figure 1.
Figure 1: The Reported Transfers Section in Case Management
The Reported Transfers section, by default, lists the currently reported outgoing fraud-suspicious
Interac
e-Transfer transactions involving your customers. The date search feature, located above the Reported Transfers section, allows an OAS Fraud Analyst to search and list fraud-suspicious transactions that
Interac
reported on previous days. Any transaction that
Interac
reports as fraud-suspicious is searchable in OAS through the Reported Transfers feature for two years from the date of the transaction.
Transactions listed under Reported Transfers can be filtered by Fraud Status, Sending FI, or Receiving FI via drop-down menus in their respective fields.
Each transaction listed under Reported Transfers contains an
Interac
Payment Reference or
Interac
Request Reference link in the Reference field. The link opens the transaction details page when selected. Listed transactions also contain a Customer ID field with a link that opens a Customer Details page when selected. See Figure 2 and Figure 3.
Figure 2: The Reference Number link to a Transfer Details Page
Figure 3: The Customer ID Link to a Customer Details Page
Once an
Interac
Payment Reference or an
Interac
Request Reference link is selected in the Reference field, the Transfer Details / Request Details page is displayed. See Figure 4.
Note:	An OAS Fraud Analyst sees details and functionality on the Transfer Details or Request Details pages not available to OAS CSR users. See Section 6.3, Transaction Details for OAS Fraud Analysts.
Figure 4: Transfer Details - OAS Fraud Analyst View
When a Customer ID link is selected in the Customer ID field, the Customer Details page is displayed. See Figure 5.
Note: 	An OAS Fraud Analyst sees details and functionality on the Customer Details page not available to OAS CSR users. See Section 6.4, Customer Details for OAS Fraud Analysts.
Figure 5: Customer Details â OAS Fraud Analyst View
6.3	Transaction Details for OAS Fraud Analysts
The Transfer Details / Request Details page for a transaction contains additional information and functionality available only to the OAS Fraud Analyst, as follows:
Sender IP Address
The Senderâs IP address is displayed in the sender details section of the page.
Recipient IP Address
The Recipientâs IP address is displayed in the recipient details section of the page. This information is only available for completed transactions. Itâs not available for transactions that have settled through Autodeposit.
Blocked Handle Indicator
An indicator displays beside a handle (e.g., an email address, mobile phone number, or account number) if the handle is on an
Interac
blocked list. See Figure 6.
Note:	Central 1 and
Interac
do not take requests to remove handles from a blocked list. All the handles are blocked automatically by
Interac
when a related transactionâs fraud status is set to Reported and are removed automatically when the transaction is marked with a fraud status of Confirmed Legit or Presumed Legit. Transfers can still be sent or received though a blocked handle, subject to
Interacâs
review.
Figure 6: Transfer Details â Blocked Handle Indicator
Fraud Status History
The Fraud Status column in the History section of a page shows when a transaction was reported by
Interac
as fraud-suspicious and when and how its fraud status was resolved by an OAS Fraud Analyst. See Figure 7.
Figure 7: Transfer Details History â Fraud Status Column
Add Transfer Memo
The Add Transfer Memo action appearing on a page opens an Add Transfer Memo pop-up form when selected. The OAS Fraud Analyst can use this form to add optional notes about a transaction as part of their suspicious activity investigation. See Figure 8 and Figure 9. Transfer memos can be viewed in the transactionâs history, as described in Section 4.4, Viewing Transfer Memos.
Figure 8: Add Transfer Memo Action
Figure 9: Add Transfer Memo Popup Form
Fraud Status Actions
A collection of actions that appears on the details page, indicating the current fraud status assigned to the transaction. The current fraud status is represented by the action in the collection that is greyed out. If no action is currently greyed out, it indicates that the fraud status of the transaction has yet to be decided. See Figure 10.
Figure 10: Fraud Status Actions
Important
: 	An OAS Fraud Analyst
shouldnât
use fraud status actions in OAS to set the fraud status of transactions if youâre using Central 1âs Enterprise Fraud Management (EFM) system. Refer to Section 6.6, Setting the Transaction Fraud Status.
Not Blocked / Blocked and Cancel
These actions on the details page of pending transactions allows an OAS Fraud Analyst to unblock or cancel the transaction. See Figure 11.
Figure 11: Transfer Details Not Blocked / Blocked and Cancel Actions
Note: 	Pending fraud-suspicious transactions reported by
Interac
may be blocked when they are reported. For FAS clients, the OAS Fraud Analyst can manually unblock a transaction in OAS using the Not Blocked action when they determine that the transaction is legitimate. For EFM clients, a transaction should be automatically unblocked by EFM when the fraud status of the transaction is updated in EFM as a legitimate transaction.
Note: 	Pending transactions determined to be either fraudulent or sent in error can be manually cancelled in OAS using the Cancel action. For FAS clients, an OAS Fraud Analyst should manually Cancel a transaction when they determine that the transaction is a fraud or scam. For EFM clients, EFM should automatically cancel a transaction determined to be fraudulent when the fraud status of the transaction is updated in EFM as a fraud or scam. For non-fraudulent transactions needing cancellation (e.g., customer-reported transfers sent in error), an OAS Fraud Analyst can manually cancel the transactions in OAS using the Cancel action.
Note:	For EFM clients, if there is a known communications interruption between EFM and
Interac
that prevents EFM from automatically
unblocking or cancelling a transaction, an OAS Fraud Analyst may use OAS to manually unblock or cancel the transaction using the Not Blocked / Blocked or Cancel actions.
6.4	Customer Details for OAS Fraud Analysts
The Customer Details page contains additional functionality available only to an OAS Fraud Analyst, as follows:
Enabled/Disabled
This action allows an OAS Fraud Analyst to disable or enable a customerâs
Interac
profile. Customers with disabled
Interac
profiles canât send or receive transfers through
Interac
e-Transfer. See Figure 12.
Figure 12: Customer Details â Enabled Action
Note:	Enabling or disabling a customerâs profile in OAS is an event that gets recorded in the Profile History tab of the Customer Details page.
Add Customer Memo
This action opens an âAdd customer memoâ pop-up form that the OAS Fraud Analyst can use to add optional notes about a customer profile as part of a suspicious activity investigation. See Figure 13 and Figure 14.
Figure 13: Customer Details â Add customer memo Action
Figure 14: Add customer memo Popup Form
Customer memos can be viewed using the View action in the Memo field of the Profile History tab. The memo appears as part of a âMEMO_ADDEDâ event recording when the memo was added and by whom.
Figure 15: A MEMO_ADDED Event on the Customer Details Profile History Tab
6.5
Investigating Fraud-Suspicious Transactions
Interac
Alerts
Interac
sends out alerts to Central 1 whenever a fraud-suspicious transaction is reported involving you as the sending or receiving financial institution. Fraud-suspicious transactions are transactions that are
scored by
Interac
as fraud-suspicious based on their fraud risk analysis,
related to another currently reported fraud-suspicious transaction whose fraud status has yet to be determined, or
related to previous transactions that were confirmed to be fraudulent.
If youâre the sending financial institution of a reported transaction, the alert is delivered to you via FAS or EFM when
Interac
reports it. The transaction may or may not have been completed when itâs reported.
If youâre the receiving financial institution of a reported transaction, the
Interac
alert email notification is forwarded to you when
Interac
identifies the receiving institution. This happens when the transaction completes or when a recipient attempts to complete it.
Interac
also sends follow-up email alerts when the fraud status of a reported transaction has been updated.
All
Interac
fraud alerts concerning you as the sending or receiving institution are re-directed by Central 1 to you. If youâre the sending financial institution, itâs your responsibility to investigate the transaction and update its fraud status as legitimate or fraudulent. If youâre the receiving financial institution, itâs your duty to assist in recovering funds if the transaction is deemed fraudulent, and to conduct a fraud review on your customerâs activity.
Outgoing
Interac
e-Transfer Transactions
On receipt of an
Interac
e-Transfer fraud alert, as the sending financial institution, you must investigate the transaction to determine if itâs legitimate or if any wrongful activity has occurred.
If a suspicious transaction has not been completed, it may be blocked by
Interac
(or by EFM in the case of Central 1 EFM clients)
pending the outcome of your investigation. If you determine that the blocked transaction is fraudulent, you can cancel it to prevent the loss of funds.
If a suspicious transaction has already completed and you determine it was fraudulent, you can initiate a Funds Recovery request through OAS to try to get back your customerâs funds. See Section 6.7, Funds Recovery Requests.
Regardless of whether the suspicious transaction is pending or completed, the transaction must be investigated and the outcome of the investigation recorded by updating the transactionâs fraud status. See Section 6.6, Setting the Transaction Fraud Status.
Itâs up to you to decide how to investigate suspicious transactions. Your investigations may involve contacting your customers to determine the legitimacy of the transactions and/or using OAS to review the transaction details and customer details associated with the transactions. Some of the new features available to the OAS Fraud Analyst may assist in your investigations (e.g., knowing the IP addresses of senders and recipients, seeing when mobile phone numbers and email addresses associated with the transfers are blocked).
Categorizing Wrongful Activity
Wrongful activity may take various forms, including:
Unauthorized Transfers
â Transfers, unauthorized by the sender, sent to unknown parties due to your customerâs banking credentials being compromised
Intercepted Transfers
â Transfers, authorized by the sender, not deposited into an account belonging to the intended recipient because the recipientâs email address or mobile phone number was hijacked
Scams
â Transfers, authorized by the sender, sent in good faith for a false product, service, or opportunity designed to defraud unsuspecting victims
Disputes
â Transfers, authorized by the sender, sent in good faith for goods or services which are subject to a disagreement between the sender and the recipient
Family/Friendly Fraud
â Transfers, not authorized by the sender, sent by a someone known to the customer who has compromised their banking credentials
Proceeds of Bad Deposit
â Transfers, authorized by the sender, using funds they donât have, obtained from false or bad deposits
First-party Fraud
â Transfers, not authorized by the sender, sent by someone known by the customer to whom they have authorized the use their account
Incoming
Interac
e-Transfer Transactions
On receipt of an
Interac
e-Transfer fraud alert, as the receiving financial institution, you must examine the transaction with heightened awareness that you may be called upon to recover funds from your customer if the transaction is determined to be fraudulent, taking whatever precautions you consider appropriate.
6.6	Setting the Transaction Fraud Status
Following the investigation of an outgoing
Interac
e-Transfer fraud-suspicious transaction, and the determination of whether the transaction is legitimate or fraudulent, the transactionâs fraud status must be updated with
Interac
. The process used to update the transaction depends on which Central 1 fraud management system your institution uses.
Financial Institutions on Enterprise Fraud Management (EFM)
If you use Central 1âs Enterprise Fraud Management (EFM) system,
Interac
-reported fraud-suspicious transactions are reported as alerted transactions in EFM. EFM must be used to mark the transactions as either legitimate or fraudulent, according to the outcome of your fraud investigations. EFM updates the
Interac
e-Transfer transaction through its interface with
Interac
when the fraud alert is actioned in EFM. Refer to the Enterprise Fraud Management Part in the Payment Solution Manual for details about actioning fraud alerts in EFM.
Note:	The fraud status actions that appear against the transaction on the Transfer Details / Request Details page in OAS
shouldnât be used
to set the fraud status of an
Interac
e-Transfer transaction alerted through EFM. See Section 6.3, Transaction Details for OAS Fraud Analysts, for more information. An exception to this is if thereâs a known communications disruption between EFM and
Interac.
Only in such cases is it acceptable to set the transaction fraud status manually in OAS.
Financial Institutions on Fraud Alert System (FAS)
If you use Central 1âs Fraud Alert System (FAS) system,
Interac
-reported fraud-suspicious transactions are logged in FAS as fraud events. Once you determine through your fraud investigation if the transaction is legitimate or fraudulent, request an update to the alertâs fraud status in FAS. At the same time, locate the transaction in OAS and manually set the transactionâs fraud status using one of the fraud status actions, as described below. See the FAS User Guide, available from Central 1âs Fraud Alert System application, for details on managing alerts in FAS.
Self-reported Frauds or Scams
If a customer reports a fraudulent transaction to you that wasnât otherwise reported as a fraud-suspicious transaction by
Interac
, an alert must be created manually for the transaction in your Central 1 fraud management system. See the Enterprise Fraud Management Part in the Payment Solution Manual for details about manually adding alerts in EFM. See the FAS User Guide, available from Central 1âs Fraud Alert System application, for details on requesting that an alert be added to FAS.
You must locate the transaction in OAS and manually set the transactionâs fraud status using one of the fraud status actions, as described next.
Manually Setting a Transaction Fraud Status in OAS
To manually set a transactionâs fraud status in OAS:
Log in to OAS as described in Section 3.5, Logging in to OAS.
Open the details page for the transaction in OAS as described in Section 6.2, Viewing Fraud-suspicious Transactions.
Select the appropriate fraud status from the fraud status actions as they appear on the transaction details page. See Figure 16. Options include:
â
Presume Legit FI
â The transaction is presumed to be legitimate.
â
Confirm Legit FI
â The transaction is confirmed to be legitimate.
â
Fraud
â The transaction is confirmed to be a fraud.
â
Scam
â The transaction is confirmed to be a scam.
Figure 16: Fraud Status Actions
Complete the appropriate fraud status details form that appears, then select
Submit
. Each variation of the details form provides a Memo field where optional notes may be added to support the fraud status selected. In the case of the fraud status Fraud or Scam, the details form also contains options to block selected email, phone, or account handles. In the case of the status Fraud, the details form also contains a Category selection field with a drop-down menu for selecting a specific type of scam. See Figure 17.
Figure 17: A Fraud Status Details Form
Note: 	After setting a transactionâs fraud status in OAS, a new FRAUD_STATUS_CHANGE event appears in the transactionâs history. If a memo was included, a separate FRAUD_MEMO_ADDED event also appears. See Figure 18.
Figure 18: Fraud Status Change Event
6.7	Funds Recovery Requests
Funds Recovery Requests Overview
When an outgoing
Interac
e-Transfer transaction has been confirmed as a fraud or scam and its fraud status has been updated accordingly, if the transactionâs status is Completed, the OAS Fraud Analyst can initiate a funds recovery request. Use the Recover Funds action that appears beside the collection of fraud status actions on the Transaction Details page. See Figure 19.
Figure 19: The Recover Funds Action
Once a funds recovery request is initiated, it is listed among other fund recovery requests in the Funds Recovery section of the Case Management page in OAS. See Figure 20. Select the Recovery ID link beside the listed request to open the Funds Recovery Details page.
Figure 20: Funds Recovery on the Case Management Page
Initiated funds recovery requests can also be viewed in the Funds Recovery tab of the Transfer Details page of a transaction. This tab is only visible when a funds recover request exists next to the transaction. See Figure 21. This tab displays a request history, showing the events logged against the request. A Recovery ID link is available in each logged event that opens the Funds Recovery Details page.
Figure 21: Funds Recovery on the Transfer Details page
Requesting a Funds Recovery
To request a funds recovery:
Log in to OAS as described in Section 3.5, Logging in to OAS.
Open the Transfer Details page for the outgoing transaction whose funds you seek to recover. See Section 6.2, Viewing Fraud-suspicious Transactions.
Select the
Recover Funds
action. This initiates a funds recovery request and opens the Funds Recover Details page. See Figure 22.
Note:	The funds recovery request is also visible in the Funds Recovery section of the Case Management page. See Figure 20 above. Also, request event logging appears on the Funds Recovery tab on the transactionâs Transfer Details page. See Figure 21 above.
Record the text âPlease confirm if there are any funds available for recovery and if available, provide email for indemnity formâ in the Memo field of the Funds Recovery Details page.
Use any of the other available actions on the page before submitting the request. Options include:
â
Add file
â Select to add any supporting evidence.
â
Continue Later
â Select to keep the initial draft request or current changes. Until the draft request is first submitted, the recipient financial institution is not notified about the request, nor can they see the request when they view the transaction on their end. A draft funds recovery request can be aborted using the Discard action.
â
Discard
â Select to delete the request. This option is only available before a fundâs recovery request is first submitted.
â
View Funds Recovery History
â Select the navigation arrow to open the Funds Recovery tab on the Transfer Details page. This displays the events logged against the request. You can return to the Funds Recover Details page using the link Recover ID link.
Select
Submit
.
Figure 22: The Funds Recovery Details page â Sending Institution
An
Interac
email notification is sent to the receiving financial institution on the successful submission of a funds recovery request.
Once it is submitted, the funds recovery request is a live form available to both the sending and receiving institutions to view and use.
The receiving financial institution must update the Funds Recovery Details page, which is displayed on the Funds Recovery tab of the source transaction in OAS. They must record the amount of the funds recovered and the contact details to be used for coordinating settlement between institutions. An
Interac
email notification is sent to the sending financial institution when the receiving financial institution responds to the request, as described below.
Responding to a Funds Recovery Request
For incoming funds recovery requests, you receive an
Interac
email notification that a funds recovery request was submitted to you from the sending financial institution. Incoming funds recovery requests appear among your outgoing funds recovery requests in the Funds Transfers section of the OAS Case Management page.
You can distinguish incoming funds recovery requests from outgoing funds recovery requests by the identities of the sending and receiving financial institutions displayed in the Funds Recovery section of the Case Management page. From there, you can view the sender and recipient details by opening the Funds Recovery Details page of the request (by selecting the
Recovery ID
link). Then select the
View Funds Recovery History
link to go to the Transfer Details page of the associated transaction.
Another way to distinguish incoming funds recovery requests from outgoing requests is to look for the appearance of the Recovered Funds field on the Funds Recovery Details page of the request. See Figure 23. The Recovered Funds field is visible only after the sending financial institution submits the funds recovery request. Also, the Submit action is disabled on the Funds Recovery Details page until an amount is entered into that field.
An OAS Fraud Analyst responds to a funds-recovery request as follows:
Log in to OAS as described in Section 3.5, Logging in to OAS.
Expand the Funds Recovery section on the Case Management page. See Figure 23.
Figure 23: The Funds Recovery Section in Case Management
Locate the incoming funds recovery request from the Funds Recovery section.
Select the
Recovery ID
link beside funds recovery request. See Figure 24.
Figure 24: The Recovery ID
Complete the Funds Recovery Details page, as shown in Figure 25, as follows:
â
Recovered Funds
â Enter the amount of funds you will return in settlement of this request.
â
Memo
â Enter any relevant notes along with an email address for coordinating institutional settlement.
Figure 25: The Funds Recovery Details Page â Receiving Institution
Select
Submit
.
Settle the funds recovery between your institutions as per your existing procedures for claims and settlements.
END
